Provable statistical accuracy with high probability through multiplicative error bounds.
Significant computational speedups in training efficiency on large-scale datasets.
Stabilization of inference through geometric approximations of the convex hull.
Demonstrated superior adaptability compared to traditional full-parametric models in complex scenarios.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop the first coreset construction for semi-parametric multivariate models to enable scalable, high-accuracy distribution estimation.

title: Text-Based Personas for Simulating User Privacy Decisions
summary: The ability to simulate human privacy decisions has significant implications for aligning autonomous agents with individual intent and conducting cost-effective, large-scale privacy-centric user studies. Prior approaches prompt Large Language Models (LLMs) with natural language user statements, data-sharing histories, or demographic attributes to simulate privacy decisions. These approaches, however, fail to balance individual-level accuracy, prompt usability, token efficiency, and population-level representation. We present Narriva, an approach that generates text-based synthetic privacy personas to address these shortcomings. Narriva grounds persona generation in prior user privacy decisions, such as those from large-scale survey datasets, rather than purely relying on demographic stereotypes. It compresses this data into concise, human-readable summaries structured by established privacy theories. Through benchmarking across five diverse datasets, we analyze the characteristics of Narriva's synthetic personas in modeling both individual and population-level privacy preferences. We find that grounding personas in past privacy behaviors achieves up to 88% predictive accuracy (significantly outperforming a non-personalized LLM baseline), and yields an 80-95% reduction in prompt tokens compared to in-context learning with raw examples. Finally, we demonstrate that personas synthesized from a single survey can reproduce the aggregate privacy behaviors and statistical distributions (TVComplement up to 0.85) of entirely different studies.

processed.summary:
• **Narriva** introduces a framework for generating synthetic, text-based privacy personas grounded in longitudinal behavioral data rather than static demographic stereotypes.
• The architecture leverages **Established Privacy Theories** to structure data compression, converting raw sharing histories into concise, human-readable summaries for context injection.
• The approach significantly optimizes **Token Efficiency**, achieving an 80-95% reduction in context size compared to standard in-context learning with raw examples.
• Impact centers on **AI Alignment**, providing a scalable method to simulate human privacy intent and improve the decision-making of autonomous LLM agents.
key_results: Achieved up to 88% predictive accuracy in individual privacy decision simulation.
Outperformed non-personalized LLM baselines across five diverse datasets.
Reduced prompt token consumption by 80-95% through behavioral summarization.
Reproduced population-level statistical distributions with a TVComplement score of 0.85.
Successfully synthesized personas from a single survey to model entirely different study populations.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Narriva, creating efficient text-based personas that accurately simulate human privacy decisions for agent alignment.

title: Embodied Science: Closing the Discovery Loop with Agentic Embodied AI
summary: Artificial intelligence has demonstrated remarkable capability in predicting scientific properties, yet scientific discovery remains an inherently physical, long-horizon pursuit governed by experimental cycles. Most current computational approaches are misaligned with this reality, framing discovery as isolated, task-specific predictions rather than continuous interaction with the physical world. Here, we argue for embodied science, a paradigm that reframes scientific discovery as a closed loop tightly coupling agentic reasoning with physical execution. We propose a unified Perception-Language-Action-Discovery (PLAD) framework, wherein embodied agents perceive experimental environments, reason over scientific knowledge, execute physical interventions, and internalize outcomes to drive subsequent exploration. By grounding computational reasoning in robust physical feedback, this approach bridges the gap between digital prediction and empirical validation, offering a roadmap for autonomous discovery systems in the life and chemical sciences.

processed.summary:
* Proposes the **PLAD (Perception-Language-Action-Discovery)** framework to transition scientific discovery from static predictions to **closed-loop agentic reasoning**.
* Integrates **multimodal perception** with domain-specific knowledge to enable agents to perform **physical interventions** and internalize experimental outcomes.
* Focuses on **long-horizon planning** and continuous feedback cycles to ground digital AI predictions in **empirical validation**.
* Targets the automation of discovery workflows in **life and chemical sciences** by coupling reasoning models with robotic execution.
key_results: Definition of 'Embodied Science' as a closed-loop paradigm for autonomous discovery.
Development of the PLAD framework unifying perception, reasoning, and action.
Shift from task-specific property prediction to continuous interaction with physical environments.
Mechanism for agents to internalize physical feedback to drive iterative experimental design.
Roadmap for bridging the gap between computational simulation and real-world laboratory execution.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose the PLAD framework to automate scientific discovery through closed-loop, agentic reasoning and physical experimentation.

title: One Model, Two Minds: Task-Conditioned Reasoning for Unified Image Quality and Aesthetic Assessment
summary: Unifying Image Quality Assessment (IQA) and Image Aesthetic Assessment (IAA) in a single multimodal large language model is appealing, yet existing methods adopt a task-agnostic recipe that applies the same reasoning strategy and reward to both tasks. We show this is fundamentally misaligned: IQA relies on low-level, objective perceptual cues and benefits from concise distortion-focused reasoning, whereas IAA requires deliberative semantic judgment and is poorly served by point-wise score regression. We identify these as a reasoning mismatch and an optimization mismatch, and provide empirical evidence for both through controlled probes. Motivated by these findings, we propose TATAR (Task-Aware Thinking with Asymmetric Rewards), a unified framework that shares the visual-language backbone while conditioning post-training on each task's nature. TATAR combines three components: fast--slow task-specific reasoning construction that pairs IQA with concise perceptual rationales and IAA with deliberative aesthetic narratives; two-stage SFT+GRPO learning that establishes task-aware behavioral priors before reward-driven refinement; and asymmetric rewards that apply Gaussian score shaping for IQA and Thurstone-style completion ranking for IAA. Extensive experiments across eight benchmarks demonstrate that TATAR consistently outperforms prior unified baselines on both tasks under in-domain and cross-domain settings, remains competitive with task-specific specialized models, and yields more stable training dynamics for aesthetic assessment. Our results establish task-conditioned post-training as a principled paradigm for unified perceptual scoring. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/yinwen2019/TATAR.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **TATAR**, a framework that decouples Image Quality Assessment (IQA) and Image Aesthetic Assessment (IAA) via **task-conditioned post-training**.
- Utilizes a **fast-slow reasoning strategy**, assigning concise perceptual rationales to objective IQA and deliberative narratives to subjective IAA.
- Leverages a **two-stage SFT+GRPO** (Group Relative Policy Optimization) learning process to establish behavioral priors followed by reward-driven reinforcement learning.
- Implements **asymmetric rewards** using Gaussian score shaping for IQA and Thurstone-style ranking for IAA to optimize task-specific alignment.
key_results: Outperforms prior unified multimodal baselines across eight diverse IQA and IAA benchmarks.
Achieves performance levels competitive with specialized, task-specific vision models.
Empirically proves that IQA benefits from concise logic while IAA thrives on deliberative, slow-thinking reasoning.
Demonstrates significantly more stable training dynamics for aesthetic assessment compared to standard score regression.
Showcases superior zero-shot generalization in cross-domain assessment scenarios through task-conditioned priors.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: East China Normal University introduces TATAR, utilizing task-aware GRPO and asymmetric rewards to unify image quality and aesthetic assessment.

title: ReManNet: A Riemannian Manifold Network for Monocular 3D Lane Detection
summary: Monocular 3D lane detection remains challenging due to depth ambiguity and weak geometric constraints. Mainstream methods rely on depth guidance, BEV projection, and anchor- or curve-based heads with simplified physical assumptions, remapping high-dimensional image features while only weakly encoding road geometry. Lacking an invariant geometric-topological coupling between lanes and the underlying road surface, 2D-to-3D lifting is ill-posed and brittle, often degenerating into concavities, bulges, and twists. To address this, we propose the Road-Manifold Assumption: the road is a smooth 2D manifold in $\mathbb{R}^3$, lanes are embedded 1D submanifolds, and sampled lane points are dense observations, thereby coupling metric and topology across surfaces, curves, and point sets. Building on this, we propose ReManNet, which first produces initial lane predictions with an image backbone and detection heads, then encodes geometry as Riemannian Gaussian descriptors on the symmetric positive-definite (SPD) manifold, and fuses these descriptors with visual features through a lightweight gate to maintain coherent 3D reasoning. We also propose the 3D Tunnel Lane IoU (3D-TLIoU) loss, a joint point-curve objective that computes slice-wise overlap of tubular neighborhoods along each lane to improve shape-level alignment. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that ReManNet achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) or competitive results. On OpenLane, it improves F1 by +8.2% over the baseline and by +1.8% over the previous best, with scenario-level gains of up to +6.6%. The code will be publicly available at https://github.com/changehome717/ReManNet.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **ReManNet**, a framework that models roads as smooth 2D manifolds and lanes as 1D submanifolds to solve depth ambiguity in monocular vision.
- Implements **Riemannian Gaussian descriptors** on the Symmetric Positive-Definite (SPD) manifold to encode geometric-topological coupling between lanes and road surfaces.
- Introduces the **3D Tunnel Lane IoU (3D-TLIoU)** loss, a joint point-curve objective that uses tubular neighborhoods to ensure better shape-level alignment.
- Fuses geometric descriptors with visual features through a **lightweight gate mechanism** to maintain coherent 3D reasoning and prevent structural artifacts like twists or bulges.
key_results: Achieved +8.2% F1 score improvement over the baseline on the OpenLane benchmark.
Surpassed the previous state-of-the-art (SOTA) by +1.8% in F1 score.
Demonstrated scenario-level performance gains of up to +6.6%.
Successfully addressed 2D-to-3D lifting failures like concavities and twists using manifold constraints.
Established a new joint point-curve objective for high-precision 3D lane reconstruction.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Beijing Institute of Technology researchers introduce ReManNet, using Riemannian geometry to achieve state-of-the-art monocular 3D lane detection performance.

title: Evaluating Image Editing with LLMs: A Comprehensive Benchmark and Intermediate-Layer Probing Approach
summary: Evaluating text-guided image editing (TIE) methods remains a challenging problem, as reliable assessment should simultaneously consider perceptual quality, alignment with textual instructions, and preservation of original image content. Despite rapid progress in TIE models, existing evaluation benchmarks remain limited in scale and often show weak correlation with human perceptual judgments. In this work, we introduce TIEdit, a benchmark for systematic evaluation of text-guided image editing methods. TIEdit consists of 512 source images paired with editing prompts across eight representative editing tasks, producing 5,120 edited images generated by ten state-of-the-art TIE models. To obtain reliable subjective ratings, 20 experts are recruited to produce 307,200 raw subjective ratings, which accumulates into 15,360 mean opinion scores (MOSs) across three evaluation dimensions: perceptual quality, editing alignment, and content preservation. Beyond the benchmark itself, we further propose EditProbe, an LLM-based evaluator that estimates editing quality via intermediate-layer probing of hidden representations. Instead of relying solely on final model outputs, EditProbe extracts informative representations from intermediate layers of multimodal large language models to better capture semantic and perceptual relationships between source images, editing instructions, and edited results. Experimental results demonstrate that widely used automatic evaluation metrics show limited correlation with human judgments on editing tasks, while EditProbe achieves substantially stronger alignment with human perception. Together, TIEdit and EditProbe provide a foundation for more reliable and perceptually aligned evaluation of text-guided image editing methods.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **TIEdit**, a large-scale benchmark for **text-guided image editing (TIE)** evaluation, featuring 512 source images and 15,360 expert-provided **Mean Opinion Scores (MOS)**.
- Proposes **EditProbe**, a novel evaluation framework that utilizes **intermediate-layer probing** of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to capture deep semantic and perceptual relationships.
- Addresses the limitations of standard metrics by assessing three distinct dimensions: **perceptual quality**, **editing alignment**, and **content preservation**.
- Demonstrates that internal hidden representations of MLLMs provide superior signals for **human-aligned evaluation** compared to traditional automated metrics or final-layer outputs.
key_results: Created a dataset of 5,120 images generated by 10 SOTA models across 8 distinct editing tasks.
Collected 307,200 raw ratings from 20 experts to ensure robust, high-quality subjective ground truth.
Proved that traditional metrics show weak correlation with human perceptual judgments in image editing contexts.
Validated that EditProbe achieves significantly higher alignment with human scores across all evaluation dimensions.
Established a comprehensive foundation for systematic evaluation of text-guided image editing methods.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce EditProbe, using MLLM intermediate-layer probing to achieve superior human-aligned evaluation of text-guided image editing.

title: FREAK: A Fine-grained Hallucination Evaluation Benchmark for Advanced MLLMs
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) suffer from hallucinations. Existing hallucination evaluation benchmarks are often limited by over-simplified tasks leading to saturated metrics, or insufficient diversity that fails to adequately assess the hallucination extent in state-of-the-art multimodal models. To address this gap, we propose FREAK, a comprehensive multimodal benchmark designed for fine-grained hallucination assessment in MLLMs. Through high-quality photorealistic images featuring fine-grained counter-commonsense edits, FREAK innovatively evaluates hallucination phenomena in detailed visual perception of MLLMs. Extensive experiments on FREAK show severe hallucination issues in SOTA models regarding detailed visual perception. To enable deeper investigation, we curate a controlled subset to indirectly evaluate the model's ability to perceive target detailed information. Through systematic evaluation of prevailing Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting techniques within this task, we reveal critical insights regarding hallucination patterns and model reasoning processes.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **FREAK**, a novel benchmark designed to evaluate **fine-grained hallucination** in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) using **photorealistic counter-commonsense images**.
- Focuses on **detailed visual perception** by manipulating image details to contradict common world knowledge, exposing model reliance on linguistic priors over visual evidence.
- Implements a **controlled subset methodology** to indirectly assess how well models perceive specific target details versus general scene context.
- Conducts a systematic evaluation of **Chain-of-Thought (CoT)** prompting to identify critical failure points in current multimodal reasoning architectures.
key_results: State-of-the-art MLLMs demonstrate severe hallucination when processing fine-grained visual details.
Traditional benchmarks are becoming saturated and fail to differentiate the performance of advanced multimodal models.
Counter-commonsense image edits effectively reveal that models often guess based on probability rather than visual grounding.
Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting provides insights into model reasoning but does not eliminate fine-grained perception errors.
The FREAK benchmark provides a more rigorous metric for evaluating the reliability of generative AI in visual tasks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce FREAK, a benchmark using counter-commonsense images to expose severe fine-grained hallucination issues in SOTA MLLMs.

title: Multimodal branched transport infers anatomically aligned brain reaction maps
summary: How external stimulation is transformed into distributed reaction patterns remains unresolved at the level of propagation architecture. Existing large-scale control models quantify transition costs on prescribed networks but do not infer the routing map itself from source and target activity. Here we combine task-related blood-oxygen-level-dependent responses, source-reconstructed electrophysiology and tractography-derived anisotropy to estimate stimulation and reaction measures, define an anatomical transport cost, and infer a branched propagation architecture by variational optimisation. Unlike standard transport formulations, branched transport favours aggregation of signal into shared neural highways before redistribution. We further attach a stochastic graph-induced dynamics to the inferred map and quantify the trade-off between geometric efficiency and dynamical controllability. We show that multimodal data generate anatomically aligned brain reaction maps, that anisotropic costs qualitatively reshape routing backbones relative to isotropic baselines, and that hybrid geometric--dynamical optimisation reveals non-trivial rank reversals across branching regimes.

processed.summary:
- Introduces a **multimodal branched transport** framework to infer routing maps of brain activity propagation from multimodal data (BOLD, electrophysiology, and tractography).
- Employs **variational optimization** to model how signal aggregation into shared "neural highways" occurs, contrasting with traditional isotropic transport models.
- Integrates **stochastic graph-induced dynamics** to quantify the fundamental trade-off between geometric efficiency and dynamical controllability in brain networks.
key_results: Branched transport models successfully identify anatomically aligned brain reaction backbones.
Anisotropic costs significantly reshape routing maps compared to traditional isotropic baselines.
The framework reveals non-trivial rank reversals in control regimes across different branching regimes.
Variational optimization effectively infers routing maps directly from source and target activity.
The model provides a new method for quantifying the balance between transport efficiency and system controllability.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers utilize multimodal branched transport to infer anatomically aligned brain reaction maps and optimize neural signal routing.

title: ConSearcher: Supporting Conversational Information Seeking in Online Communities with Member Personas
summary: Many people browse online communities to learn from others' experiences and opinions, e.g., for constructing travel plans. Conversational search powered by large language models (LLMs) could ease this information-seeking task, but it remains under-investigated within the online community. In this paper, we first conducted an exploratory study (N=10) that indicated the helpfulness of a classic conversational search tool and identified room for improvement. Then, we proposed ConSearcher, an LLM-powered tool with dynamically generated member personas based on user queries to facilitate conversational search in the community. In ConSearcher, users can clarify their interests by checking what a simulated member similar to them may ask and get responses from diverse members' perspectives. A within-subjects study (N=27) showed that compared to two conversational search baselines, ConSearcher led to significantly higher information-seeking outcome and user engagement but raised concerns about over-personalization. We discuss implications for supporting conversational information seeking in online communities.

processed.summary:
• Proposes **ConSearcher**, an LLM-driven architecture that utilizes **dynamic persona generation** to facilitate conversational information seeking within online community datasets.
• Implements a multi-agent framework where the system generates **simulated member personas** based on user queries to provide diverse, multi-perspective responses instead of single-point answers.
• Features a query-refinement mechanism allowing users to interact with **simulated counterparts** to clarify intent and discover latent information needs.
• Integrates **RAG-based grounding** by anchoring persona responses in existing community experiences and opinions to ensure factual relevance.
key_results: ConSearcher achieved significantly higher information-seeking outcomes compared to standard conversational search baselines.
User engagement metrics showed a marked increase when interacting with simulated member personas.
Dynamic persona generation helped users surface diverse perspectives that are often lost in aggregated LLM summaries.
The clarification mechanism using simulated similar-member queries successfully reduced ambiguity in user requests.
Study participants (N=27) identified 'over-personalization' as a potential risk that could limit exposure to serendipitous information.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduced ConSearcher, leveraging dynamic LLM member personas to enhance conversational search and information retrieval in online communities.

title: Invariant quantile regression for heterogeneous environments
summary: In this paper, we propose an invariant quantile regression (IQR) framework specifically designed for multi-environment datasets, which captures the invariance across different environments. This model is closely related to transfer learning, causal inference, and fair machine learning, and is motivated by scenarios in which the conditional probability of the response given covariates varies, while certain key features remain invariant. This perspective differs notably from previous works that restrict attention to the conditional mean, which is often insufficient in heterogeneous environments and the resulting estimators can become sensitive to ``bad" environments or changes in noise distributional shape. In contrast, quantile-based invariance naturally accommodates heterogeneity, and aligns more closely with structural causal models, in which variables invariant across environments at one or multiple quantile levels naturally indicate potential and stable causal predictors. Moreover, the set of endogenous variables under the IQR framework can be larger than that under the conditional mean framework typically, which in turn promotes more effective exclusion of spurious (no-causal) predictors provided that endogenous variables are not incorporated. To achieve this, we introduce a Kernel-Smoothed Focused Invariance Quantile Regression (KSFIQR) estimator, which leverages the underlying invariance structure and heterogeneity among environments, ensuring stable estimation across multiple environments. We establish the causal discovery properties of our method, demonstrate its ability to overcome the ``curse of endogeneity", and derive an $\ell_2$ error bound for our estimator in the low-dimensional regime, all in a non-asymptotic framework. From an algorithmic perspective, we implement the L-BFGS-B method and the Gumbel trick, with our numerical studies validating the proposed approach.

processed.summary:
• Introduces the **Invariant Quantile Regression (IQR)** framework to maintain model stability across diverse datasets where conditional response probabilities vary.
 • Proposes the **Kernel-Smoothed Focused Invariance Quantile Regression (KSFIQR)** estimator, which utilizes structural causal models to identify invariant features rather than just conditional means.
 • Employs the **L-BFGS-B method** and the **Gumbel trick** to optimize the detection of stable causal predictors while effectively excluding spurious, non-causal variables.
 • Establishes a **non-asymptotic framework** with an l2 error bound, ensuring the method remains robust against noise distributional changes and "bad" environments.
key_results: Development of IQR which captures invariance at multiple quantile levels to improve out-of-distribution robustness.
Proven ability to overcome the 'curse of endogeneity' by leveraging quantile-based invariance structures.
Derivation of a non-asymptotic l2 error bound for the KSFIQR estimator in low-dimensional regimes.
Demonstrated superior causal discovery properties compared to traditional conditional mean-based invariance methods.
Successful algorithmic implementation using kernel smoothing to allow for differentiable optimization in heterogeneous settings.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a Kernel-Smoothed Invariant Quantile Regression framework to enhance model stability and causal discovery in heterogeneous environments.

title: Rethinking Ground Truth: A Case Study on Human Label Variation in MLLM Benchmarking
summary: Human Label Variation (HLV), i.e. systematic differences among annotators' judgments, remains underexplored in benchmarks despite rapid progress in large language model (LLM) development. We address this gap by introducing an evaluation protocol for multimodal large language model (MLLM) benchmarking that explicitly accounts for two conditions: (1) human label agreement and (2) disagreement. We apply this protocol to two state-of-the-art MLLM families (Gemma 3, Qwen 2.5 VL) using non-aggregated human annotations from a social media content classification dataset. Across tasks, we find that larger models tend to perform best on high-agreement subsets, yet often underperform medium-sized models when human disagreement is high, indicating that parameter count alone does not determine sensitivity to ambiguity and subjectivity. These results show that benchmarks based solely on consensus labels can overstate model capabilities in such domains and that incorporating human label variation yields more realistic and robust assessments of MLLMs in content moderation pipelines.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a novel evaluation protocol for **Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs)** that bifurcates benchmarks based on **Human Label Variation (HLV)**.
- Investigates the performance of **Gemma 3** and **Qwen 2.5 VL** families across varying levels of human annotator agreement.
- Identifies a critical gap where **parameter scaling** does not necessarily improve model performance on tasks involving **subjective or ambiguous** content.
- Highlights how current **aggregated consensus labels** inflate model capability metrics in real-world application pipelines like content moderation.
key_results: Large models dominate performance on high-agreement human label subsets.
Medium-sized models often outperform larger variants in high-disagreement (subjective) scenarios.
Model parameter count is not a reliable predictor of sensitivity to human subjectivity.
Standard benchmarks overstate model reliability by ignoring the 'long tail' of human disagreement.
Non-aggregated human annotations are essential for robust evaluation of MLLM content moderation pipelines.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers reveal that larger MLLMs struggle with subjective human disagreement despite excelling on consensus-based benchmarks.

title: Dual Path Attribution: Efficient Attribution for SwiGLU-Transformers through Layer-Wise Target Propagation
summary: Understanding the internal mechanisms of transformer-based large language models (LLMs) is crucial for their reliable deployment and effective operation. While recent efforts have yielded a plethora of attribution methods attempting to balance faithfulness and computational efficiency, dense component attribution remains prohibitively expensive. In this work, we introduce Dual Path Attribution (DPA), a novel framework that faithfully traces information flow on the frozen transformer in one forward and one backward pass without requiring counterfactual examples. DPA analytically decomposes and linearizes the computational structure of the SwiGLU Transformers into distinct pathways along which it propagates a targeted unembedding vector to receive the effective representation at each residual position. This target-centric propagation achieves O(1) time complexity with respect to the number of model components, scaling to long input sequences and dense component attribution. Extensive experiments on standard interpretability benchmarks demonstrate that DPA achieves state-of-the-art faithfulness and unprecedented efficiency compared to existing baselines.

processed.summary:
• **Dual Path Attribution (DPA)** introduces a novel framework for tracing information flow in **SwiGLU-Transformers** using a single forward and backward pass.
• The architecture **analytically decomposes and linearizes** the transformer's computational structure into distinct pathways to receive effective representations at each residual position.
• DPA achieves **O(1) time complexity** relative to the number of model components, enabling dense attribution for large-scale models and long input sequences without counterfactuals.
• The method significantly improves **interpretability faithfulness** compared to existing baselines, providing a scalable solution for understanding internal model mechanisms.
key_results: Achieves O(1) complexity relative to the number of model components for attribution tasks.
Eliminates the requirement for computationally expensive counterfactual examples.
Traces information flow accurately through the non-linear SwiGLU activation functions.
Demonstrates state-of-the-art faithfulness on standard interpretability benchmarks.
Scales efficiently to dense component attribution for models with very long input sequences.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Dual Path Attribution to enable efficient, O(1) complexity interpretability and attribution for SwiGLU-based transformer architectures.

title: FedPDPO: Federated Personalized Direct Preference Optimization for Large Language Model Alignment
summary: Aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences in federated learning (FL) is challenging due to decentralized, privacy-sensitive, and highly non-IID preference data. Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) offers an efficient alternative to reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF), but its direct application in FL suffers from severe performance degradation under non-IID data and limited generalization of implicit rewards. To bridge this gap, we propose FedPDPO (Federated Personalized Direct Preference Optimization), a personalized federated framework for preference alignment of LLMs. It adopts a parameter-efficient fine-tuning architecture where each client maintains a frozen pretrained LLM backbone augmented with a Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapter, enabling communication-efficient aggregation. To address non-IID heterogeneity, we devise (1) the globally shared LoRA adapter with the personalized client-specific LLM head. Moreover, we introduce (2) a personalized DPO training strategy with a client-specific explicit reward head to complement implicit rewards and further alleviate non-IID heterogeneity, and (3) a bottleneck adapter to balance global and local features. We provide theoretical analysis establishing the probabilistic foundation and soundness. Extensive experiments on multiple preference datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, achieving up to 4.80% average accuracy improvements in federated intra-domain and cross-domain settings.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **FedPDPO**, a personalized federated framework for aligning LLMs with human preferences across decentralized, **non-IID data** sources.
- Employs a **PEFT architecture** combining a frozen pretrained backbone with a globally shared **LoRA adapter** and client-specific **personalized heads**.
- Incorporates an **explicit reward head** to complement the implicit rewards of DPO, effectively stabilizing training in heterogeneous environments.
- Utilizes a **bottleneck adapter** to manage the trade-off between global knowledge and local feature representation for superior generalization.
key_results: Achieved up to 4.80% average accuracy improvement in federated intra-domain and cross-domain settings.
Successfully mitigated the performance degradation DPO suffers under non-IID data distributions.
Established a sound probabilistic foundation through comprehensive theoretical analysis.
Demonstrated communication-efficient alignment by aggregating only LoRA adapters rather than full model weights.
Validated that a dual reward mechanism (implicit + explicit) enhances preference learning robustness.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose FedPDPO, a personalized federated DPO framework that improves LLM alignment accuracy on non-IID datasets.

title: PoC: Performance-oriented Context Compression for Large Language Models via Performance Prediction
summary: While context compression can mitigate the growing inference costs of Large Language Models (LLMs) by shortening contexts, existing methods that specify a target compression ratio or length suffer from unpredictable performance degradation, hindering their reliable deployment. We introduce a paradigm shift to Performance-oriented Context Compression (PoC), where developers specify an acceptable performance floor instead of a compression ratio. PoC employs a lightweight performance predictor to automatically find the most aggressive compression ratio that satisfies this constraint before steering an off-the-shelf compressor. We design and compare two predictor variants: a simple context-agnostic predictor and a more sophisticated context-aware one that considers the input's inherent compressibility. On both question-answering and summarization benchmarks, the context-aware predictor consistently achieves lower performance prediction error than the context-agnostic predictor, while the resulting context-aware PoC attains a superior overall performance. Our work paves the way for a more reliable, efficient, and performance-aware deployment of context compression for LLMs.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **Performance-oriented Context Compression (PoC)**, a paradigm shift that allows developers to set a **performance floor** instead of a fixed compression ratio.
- Employs a **lightweight performance predictor** to dynamically calculate the maximum allowable compression for each specific input before execution.
- Compares **context-agnostic** versus **context-aware** predictor architectures, finding that accounting for input compressibility reduces prediction error.
- Aims to solve the problem of **unpredictable performance degradation** in existing context shortening methods for more reliable LLM deployment.
key_results: PoC shifts context management from arbitrary ratios to performance-driven constraints.
Context-aware predictors consistently achieve lower performance prediction errors than agnostic models.
The framework effectively maintains user-defined performance floors across QA and summarization tasks.
Context-aware PoC achieves superior overall compression-to-performance efficiency.
Enables more aggressive compression without compromising output quality in production environments.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Researchers introduce PoC to dynamically compress LLM context based on user-defined performance floors instead of arbitrary fixed ratios.

title: Helix: A Dual-Helix Co-Evolutionary Multi-Agent System for Prompt Optimization and Question Reformulation
summary: Automated prompt optimization (APO) aims to improve large language model performance by refining prompt instructions. However, existing methods are largely constrained by fixed prompt templates, limited search spaces, or single-sided optimization that treats user questions as immutable inputs. In practice, question formulation and prompt design are inherently interdependent: clearer question structures facilitate focused reasoning and task understanding, while effective prompts reveal better ways to organize and restate queries. Ignoring this coupling fundamentally limits the effectiveness and adaptability of current APO approaches. We propose a unified multi-agent system (Helix) that jointly optimizes question reformulation and prompt instructions through a structured three-stage co-evolutionary framework. Helix integrates (1) planner-guided decomposition that breaks optimization into coupled question-prompt objectives, (2) dual-track co-evolution where specialized agents iteratively refine and critique each other to produce complementary improvements, and (3) strategy-driven question generation that instantiates high-quality reformulations for robust inference. Extensive experiments on 12 benchmarks against 6 strong baselines demonstrate the effectiveness of Helix, achieving up to 3.95% performance improvements across tasks with favorable optimization efficiency.

* **Helix** introduces a unified **multi-agent system** that co-optimizes both **prompt instructions** and **user question formulation**, addressing the limitations of fixed-input optimization.
* The architecture utilizes a **three-stage co-evolutionary framework** featuring planner-guided decomposition and dual-track agents that iteratively critique and refine each other.
* The system treats question structure and prompts as **interdependent variables**, allowing for clearer query organization and more robust reasoning during inference.
* Experimental results across 12 benchmarks show that this **strategy-driven approach** significantly outperforms traditional single-sided prompt optimization baselines.

Achieved performance gains of up to 3.95% over current state-of-the-art APO methods.
Demonstrated that joint optimization of questions and prompts is superior to optimizing prompts in isolation.
Maintained high optimization efficiency through its structured three-stage co-evolutionary framework.
Validated that strategy-driven question generation facilitates significantly better task understanding for LLMs.

title: BALM: A Model-Agnostic Framework for Balanced Multimodal Learning under Imbalanced Missing Rates
summary: Learning from multiple modalities often suffers from imbalance, where information-rich modalities dominate optimization while weaker or partially missing modalities contribute less. This imbalance becomes severe in realistic settings with imbalanced missing rates (IMR), where each modality is absent with different probabilities, distorting representation learning and gradient dynamics. We revisit this issue from a training-process perspective and propose BALM, a model-agnostic plug-in framework to achieve balanced multimodal learning under IMR. The framework comprises two complementary modules: the Feature Calibration Module (FCM), which recalibrates unimodal features using global context to establish a shared representation basis across heterogeneous missing patterns; the Gradient Rebalancing Module (GRM), which balances learning dynamics across modalities by modulating gradient magnitudes and directions from both distributional and spatial perspectives. BALM can be seamlessly integrated into diverse backbones, including multimodal emotion recognition (MER) models, without altering their architectures. Experimental results across multiple MER benchmarks confirm that BALM consistently enhances robustness and improves performance under diverse missing and imbalance settings. Code available at: https://github.com/np4s/BALM_CVPR2026.git

processed.summary:
• Introduces **BALM**, a model-agnostic plug-in framework designed to address the challenge of **Imbalanced Missing Rates (IMR)** in multimodal learning.
• Employs a **Feature Calibration Module (FCM)** to recalibrate unimodal features using global context, establishing a shared representation basis across heterogeneous data patterns.
• Utilizes a **Gradient Rebalancing Module (GRM)** to modulate gradient magnitudes and directions, preventing information-rich modalities from dominating optimization.
• Enables seamless integration into existing backbones like **Multimodal Emotion Recognition (MER)** without requiring architectural modifications.
key_results: Mitigates optimization dominance of strong modalities in imbalanced scenarios.
Improves robustness across multiple Multimodal Emotion Recognition benchmarks.
Establishes a shared representation basis despite varying missing data probabilities.
Balances learning dynamics from both distributional and spatial gradient perspectives.
Demonstrates consistent performance gains without altering base model architectures.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: The Research Team introduces BALM to balance multimodal learning through feature calibration and gradient rebalancing under imbalanced missing rates.

title: Stepwise: Neuro-Symbolic Proof Search for Automated Systems Verification
summary: Formal verification via interactive theorem proving is increasingly used to ensure the correctness of critical systems, yet constructing large proof scripts remains highly manual and limits scalability. Advances in large language models (LLMs), especially in mathematical reasoning, make their integration into software verification increasingly promising. This paper introduces a neuro-symbolic proof generation framework designed to automate proof search for systems-level verification projects. The framework performs a best-first tree search over proof states, repeatedly querying an LLM for the next candidate proof step. On the neural side, we fine-tune LLMs using datasets of proof state-step pairs; on the symbolic side, we incorporate a range of ITP tools to repair rejected steps, filter and rank proof states, and automatically discharge subgoals when search progress stalls. This synergy enables data-efficient LLM adaptation and semantics-informed pruning of the search space. We implement the framework on a new Isabelle REPL that exposes fine-grained proof states and automation tools, and evaluate it on the FVEL seL4 benchmark and additional Isabelle developments. On seL4, the system proves up to 77.6\% of the theorems, substantially surpassing previous LLM-based approaches and standalone Sledgehammer, while solving significantly more multi-step proofs. Results across further benchmarks demonstrate strong generalization, indicating a viable path toward scalable automated software verification.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **Stepwise**, a **neuro-symbolic framework** that integrates fine-tuned LLMs with Interactive Theorem Proving (ITP) tools for automated systems-level verification.
- Employs a **best-first tree search** architecture where LLMs suggest candidate proof steps while symbolic tools provide feedback to repair, filter, and rank states.
- Features a custom **Isabelle REPL** interface that enables fine-grained state interaction and automated subgoal discharge via tools like Sledgehammer.
- Demonstrates high data efficiency by combining **supervised fine-tuning** on proof state-step pairs with semantics-informed pruning of the search space.
key_results: Achieved a 77.6% proof success rate on the FVEL seL4 benchmark.
Outperformed standalone Sledgehammer and previous state-of-the-art LLM-based verification approaches.
Successfully solved a significantly higher volume of complex multi-step proofs compared to baselines.
Enabled data-efficient LLM adaptation through fine-tuning on specialized proof state-step datasets.
Demonstrated strong generalization capabilities across multiple diverse Isabelle developments.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Stepwise researchers introduce a neuro-symbolic framework that automates complex software verification with a 77.6 percent success rate.

title: LoopRPT: Reinforcement Pre-Training for Looped Language Models
summary: Looped language models (LoopLMs) perform iterative latent computation to refine internal representations, offering a promising alternative to explicit chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning. However, existing reinforcement learning (RL) paradigms primarily target output tokens, creating a structural mismatch with looped architectures whose reasoning unfolds implicitly. In this work, we propose LoopRPT, a reinforcement pre-training framework tailored for LoopLMs. By reframing next-token prediction as a next-token reasoning task, LoopRPT assigns reinforcement signals directly to latent steps using an EMA teacher reference and noisy latent rollouts. This formulation enables RL to directly shape intermediate representations, compressing effective reasoning into fewer iterations. We instantiate LoopRPT on the Ouro architecture across multiple model scales. Results demonstrate that LoopRPT consistently improves per-step representation quality, achieving Pareto dominance in accuracy-computation trade-offs. Notably, significant gains on hard tokens indicate that LoopRPT enhances early-stage reasoning rather than merely encouraging premature exits. Our findings highlight reinforcement pre-training as a principled paradigm for learning efficient latent reasoning in LoopLMs.

processed.summary:
• **LoopRPT** is a reinforcement pre-training framework designed specifically for **Looped Language Models (LoopLMs)** like the Ouro architecture.
• It reframes standard next-token prediction as a **next-token reasoning task**, applying reinforcement signals directly to **latent computation steps**.
• The framework utilizes an **EMA teacher reference** and noisy rollouts to shape intermediate representations, optimizing the model for faster reasoning convergence.
• The method bridges the gap between **implicit latent reasoning** and RL paradigms that traditionally only target final output tokens.
key_results: Achieved Pareto dominance in accuracy-computation trade-offs compared to baseline looped models.
Consistent improvement in representation quality per computation step across various model scales.
Significant performance boosts on 'hard tokens,' indicating stronger deep-reasoning capabilities.
Validated that gains result from improved early-stage reasoning rather than simple early-exit triggers.
Demonstrated that RL can successfully compress high-quality reasoning logic into fewer iterative cycles.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Tsinghua University researchers introduce LoopRPT to optimize latent reasoning in looped models, achieving superior accuracy-efficiency trade-offs via reinforcement pre-training.

title: EvoTaxo: Building and Evolving Taxonomy from Social Media Streams
summary: Constructing taxonomies from social media corpora is challenging because posts are short, noisy, semantically entangled, and temporally dynamic. Existing taxonomy induction methods are largely designed for static corpora and often struggle to balance robustness, scalability, and sensitivity to evolving discourse. We propose EvoTaxo, a LLM-based framework for building and evolving taxonomies from temporally ordered social media streams. Rather than clustering raw posts directly, EvoTaxo converts each post into a structured draft action over the current taxonomy, accumulates structural evidence over time windows, and consolidates candidate edits through dual-view clustering that combines semantic similarity with temporal locality. A refinement-and-arbitration procedure then selects reliable edits before execution, while each node maintains a concept memory bank to preserve semantic boundaries over time. Experiments on two Reddit corpora show that EvoTaxo produces more balanced taxonomies than baselines, with clearer post-to-leaf assignment, better corpus coverage at comparable taxonomy size, and stronger structural quality. A case study on the Reddit community /r/ICE_Raids further shows that EvoTaxo captures meaningful temporal shifts in discourse. Our codebase is available here.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **EvoTaxo**, an LLM-driven framework that treats taxonomy construction as a dynamic evolution process rather than a static clustering task.
- Implements a **dual-view clustering** mechanism that merges semantic similarity with **temporal locality**, ensuring new taxonomy nodes reflect evolving discourse trends.
- Features a **concept memory bank** and an **arbitration procedure** to maintain semantic integrity and prevent drift across streaming social media data.
- Demonstrates improved **corpus coverage** and structural balance on dynamic Reddit datasets compared to traditional induction baselines.
key_results: Achieved more balanced taxonomies with clearer post-to-leaf assignments than static baselines.
Provided better corpus coverage at a comparable taxonomy size across Reddit corpora.
Successfully captured meaningful temporal shifts in social discourse (e.g., /r/ICE_Raids case study).
Effective noise reduction in short social media posts via draft actions and structural evidence.
Maintained semantic boundaries over time using a specialized concept memory bank for each node.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv authors introduce EvoTaxo, an LLM-driven framework that builds and evolves dynamic taxonomies from noisy social media streams.

title: AIGQ: An End-to-End Hybrid Generative Architecture for E-commerce Query Recommendation
summary: Pre-search query recommendation, widely known as HintQ on Taobao's homepage, plays a vital role in intent capture and demand discovery, yet traditional methods suffer from shallow semantics, poor cold-start performance and low serendipity due to reliance on ID-based matching and co-click heuristics. To overcome these challenges, we propose AIGQ (AI-Generated Query architecture), the first end-to-end generative framework for HintQ scenario. AIGQ is built upon three core innovations spanning training paradigm, policy optimization and deployment architecture. First, we propose Interest-Aware List Supervised Fine-Tuning (IL-SFT), a list-level supervised learning approach that constructs training samples through session-aware behavior aggregation and interest-guided re-ranking strategy to faithfully model nuanced user intent. Accordingly, we design Interest-aware List Group Relative Policy Optimization (IL-GRPO), a novel policy gradient algorithm with a dual-component reward mechanism that jointly optimizes individual query relevance and global list properties, enhanced by a model-based reward from the online click-through rate (CTR) ranking model. To deploy under strict real-time and low-latency requirements, we further develop a hybrid offline-online architecture comprising AIGQ-Direct for nearline personalized user-to-query generation and AIGQ-Think, a reasoning-enhanced variant that produces trigger-to-query mappings to enrich interest diversity. Extensive offline evaluations and large-scale online A/B experiments on Taobao demonstrate that AIGQ consistently delivers substantial improvements in key business metrics across platform effectiveness and user engagement.

processed.summary:
* Proposes **AIGQ**, the first end-to-end generative framework for e-commerce pre-search query recommendation, addressing limitations of traditional ID-matching and co-click heuristics.
* Implements **Interest-Aware List Supervised Fine-Tuning (IL-SFT)** and **Interest-aware List Group Relative Policy Optimization (IL-GRPO)** to optimize both individual query relevance and global list properties using a CTR model-based reward.
* Features a hybrid deployment architecture consisting of **AIGQ-Direct** for personalized nearline generation and **AIGQ-Think**, a reasoning-enhanced variant for mapping triggers to diverse user interests.
* Successfully deployed on **Taobao**'s homepage (HintQ), significantly improving intent capture, cold-start performance, and serendipity through a generative approach.
key_results: Transitioned Taobao's HintQ from a retrieval-based system to a fully generative end-to-end architecture.
Developed IL-GRPO, a novel policy gradient algorithm that leverages online ranking models for RLHF-style optimization.
AIGQ-Think provides reasoning-based intent expansion to solve the 'low serendipity' problem in traditional search.
Hybrid offline-online deployment satisfies strict sub-second real-time e-commerce latency requirements.
Validated through large-scale A/B experiments showing substantial gains in platform CTR and user engagement.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Alibaba Taobao introduces AIGQ, a generative architecture using GRPO and reasoning models to optimize real-time e-commerce query recommendations.

title: WorldAgents: Can Foundation Image Models be Agents for 3D World Models?
summary: Given the remarkable ability of 2D foundation image models to generate high-fidelity outputs, we investigate a fundamental question: do 2D foundation image models inherently possess 3D world model capabilities? To answer this, we systematically evaluate multiple state-of-the-art image generation models and Vision-Language Models (VLMs) on the task of 3D world synthesis. To harness and benchmark their potential implicit 3D capability, we propose an agentic framing to facilitate 3D world generation. Our approach employs a multi-agent architecture: a VLM-based director that formulates prompts to guide image synthesis, a generator that synthesizes new image views, and a VLM-backed two-step verifier that evaluates and selectively curates generated frames from both 2D image and 3D reconstruction space. Crucially, we demonstrate that our agentic approach provides coherent and robust 3D reconstruction, producing output scenes that can be explored by rendering novel views. Through extensive experiments across various foundation models, we demonstrate that 2D models do indeed encapsulate a grasp of 3D worlds. By exploiting this understanding, our method successfully synthesizes expansive, realistic, and 3D-consistent worlds.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **multi-agent architecture** that orchestrates 2D image generators and VLMs to synthesize high-fidelity **3D world models**.
- Employs a **VLM-based Director** for prompt strategy and a **two-step Verifier** to evaluate spatial consistency in both image and reconstruction domains.
- Validates that **agentic curation** allows 2D foundation models to generate coherent, expansive scenes navigable via novel view rendering.
key_results: Confirms 2D foundation models inherently encapsulate 3D world model capabilities.
Proposed multi-agent system produces highly coherent and robust 3D reconstructions.
Achieves successful synthesis of realistic scenes suitable for novel view rendering.
Demonstrates that VLM-backed verification significantly improves 3D consistency.
Provides a systematic benchmarking framework for evaluating 3D potential in existing image models.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Stanford University researchers demonstrate that multi-agent coordination transforms 2D foundation models into consistent and explorative 3D world generators.

title: Regret Analysis of Sleeping Competing Bandits
summary: The Competing Bandits framework is a recently emerging area that integrates multi-armed bandits in online learning with stable matching in game theory. While conventional models assume that all players and arms are constantly available, in real-world problems, their availability can vary arbitrarily over time. In this paper, we formulate this setting as Sleeping Competing Bandits. To analyze this problem, we naturally extend the regret definition used in existing competing bandits and derive regret bounds for the proposed model. We propose an algorithm that simultaneously achieves an asymptotic regret bound of $\mathrm{O}\left(NK\log T_{i}/Δ^2\right)$ under reasonable assumptions, where $N$ is the number of players, $K$ is the number of arms, $T_{i}$ is the number of rounds of each player $p_i$, and $Δ$ is the minimum reward gap. We also provide a regret lower bound of $\mathrmΩ\left( N(K-N+1)\log T_{i}/Δ^2 \right)$ under the same assumptions. This implies that our algorithm is asymptotically optimal in the regime where the number of arms $K$ is relatively larger than the number of players $N$.

processed.summary:
- Introduces the **Sleeping Competing Bandits** framework, which integrates multi-armed bandits with stable matching game theory to handle dynamic player and arm availability.
- Extends standard **regret definitions** to accommodate real-world scenarios where system components may go offline or vary arbitrarily over time.
- Proposes a decentralized algorithm that achieves an **asymptotic regret bound** of O(NK log T / delta^2), ensuring efficient learning in competitive environments.
- Establishes a **regret lower bound**, proving the proposed algorithm is asymptotically optimal when the number of arms is significantly larger than the number of players.
key_results: Formulated the Sleeping Competing Bandits model for non-stationary player/arm availability.
Proposed a novel regret definition for competitive bandits in dynamic settings.
Derived an upper regret bound of O(NK log T / delta^2) for the proposed algorithm.
Established a lower regret bound of Omega(N(K-N+1) log T / delta^2).
Validated asymptotic optimality in regimes where K is relatively larger than N.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers establish optimal regret bounds for Sleeping Competing Bandits, improving multi-agent coordination under varying resource availability.

title: From Token to Item: Enhancing Large Language Models for Recommendation via Item-aware Attention Mechanism
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently gained increasing attention in the field of recommendation. Existing LLM-based methods typically represent items as token sequences, and apply attention layers on these tokens to generate recommendations. However, by inheriting the standard attention mechanism, these methods focus on modeling token-level relations. This token-centric focus overlooks the item as the fundamental unit of recommendation, preventing existing methods from effectively capturing collaborative relations at the item level. In this work, we revisit the role of tokens in LLM-driven recommendation and categorize their relations into two types: (1) intra-item token relations, which present the content semantics of an item, e.g., name, color, and size; and (2) inter-item token relations, which encode collaborative relations across items. Building on these insights, we propose a novel framework with an item-aware attention mechanism (IAM) to enhance LLMs for recommendation. Specifically, IAM devises two complementary attention layers: (1) an intra-item attention layer, which restricts attention to tokens within the same item, modeling item content semantics; and (2) an inter-item attention layer, which attends exclusively to token relations across items, capturing item collaborative relations. Through this stacked design, IAM explicitly emphasizes items as the fundamental units in recommendation, enabling LLMs to effectively exploit item-level collaborative relations. Extensive experiments on several public datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of IAM in enhancing LLMs for personalized recommendation.

processed.summary:
- Proposes the **Item-aware Attention Mechanism (IAM)** to redefine the fundamental unit of LLM-based recommendation from individual tokens to discrete items.
- Introduces an **Intra-item attention layer** that isolates token interactions within a single item to preserve and model content semantics like name and attributes.
- Features an **Inter-item attention layer** designed to capture collaborative signals by focusing exclusively on token relations across different items in a sequence.
- The stacked architecture allows LLMs to effectively exploit **item-level collaborative relations** while maintaining high-fidelity semantic understanding of item content.
key_results: Identifies token-level attention as a bottleneck for capturing collaborative filtering signals in LLMs.
Decouples intra-item semantics from inter-item collaborative dependencies through a dual-layer attention design.
Enables LLMs to treat items as fundamental units rather than arbitrary token sequences.
Outperforms standard LLM-based recommendation baselines on multiple public datasets.
Demonstrates enhanced personalized recommendation capabilities by explicitly modeling item-level relations.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce an Item-aware Attention Mechanism to improve LLM recommendation accuracy by modeling discrete item-level collaborative relations.

title: DataProphet: Demystifying Supervision Data Generalization in Multimodal LLMs
summary: Conventional wisdom for selecting supervision data for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) is to prioritize datasets that appear similar to the target benchmark, such as text-intensive or vision-centric tasks. However, it remains unclear whether such intuitive similarity reliably predicts downstream performance gains. In this work, we take a first step toward answering a practical question: can we estimate the influence of a training dataset on a target benchmark before any training is performed? To investigate this question, we conduct an in-depth analysis of transfer across 14 vision-language datasets spanning 7 diverse tasks. Our results show that intuitive task similarity is an unreliable predictor of transferability, and that generalization depends more on the specific dataset than on its broad task category. Motivated by this finding, we propose DATAPROPHET, a simple and effective training-free metric that combines multimodal perplexity, similarity, and data diversity. Experiments show that DATAPROPHET produces supervision-data rankings that strongly correlate with rankings based on actual post-training performance gains, achieving a Kendall's tau of 86.0%. Moreover, DATAPROPHET enables better supervision-data selection, yielding up to 6.9% improvement over uniform selection, 1.4% over a state-of-the-art training-based baseline, and 0.2% above oracle selection based on experimental performance. Our code and data will be released.

processed.summary:
• Proposes **DATAPROPHET**, a training-free metric designed to estimate the influence of supervision datasets on **Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs)** performance prior to training.
• Challenges the conventional wisdom that intuitive **task similarity** (e.g., text-intensive vs. vision-centric) predicts downstream gains, finding dataset-specific characteristics to be more influential.
• The framework integrates **multimodal perplexity**, semantic similarity, and **data diversity** to rank potential training data based on predicted transferability to target benchmarks.
• Provides a computationally efficient alternative to training-based selection methods, significantly optimizing the **Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT)** pipeline for multimodal tasks.
key_results: Intuitive task similarity is proven to be an unreliable predictor of model transferability across 14 vision-language datasets.
DATAPROPHET achieves a Kendall's tau of 86.0%, indicating strong correlation with actual post-training performance gains.
Data selection using the metric yields a 6.9% improvement over uniform data selection methods.
Outperformed a state-of-the-art training-based baseline by 1.4% while remaining training-free.
The metric achieved performance 0.2% above oracle selection based on actual experimental benchmarks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce DataProphet to predict multimodal dataset transferability using training-free metrics, significantly improving supervision data selection efficiency.

title: Diminishing Returns in Expanding Generative Models and Godel-Tarski-Lob Limits
summary:
Modern generative modelling systems are increasingly improved by expanding model capacity, training data, and computational resources. While empirical studies have documented such scaling behaviour across architectures including generative adversarial networks, variational autoencoders, transformer-based models, and diffusion models, the theoretical limits of capability growth in expanding generative systems remain poorly understood.
  In this paper we develop a general task-space framework for analysing expanding generative reasoning systems. Each system induces a subset of a global task space representing the tasks it can successfully solve, and system capability is measured by the probability mass of this solved-task set under a fixed task distribution. Within this framework we prove a structural result showing that, under mild assumptions, the marginal improvement in solved tasks must converge to zero as system capacity increases. Thus expanding generative systems may continue to gain capability, but the probability mass of newly solvable tasks necessarily diminishes asymptotically.
  We further provide a prediction-theoretic refinement based on complexity-weighted hypothesis classes inspired by algorithmic probability, yielding quantitative bounds on marginal improvement in prediction settings. Finally, we examine logical reasoning tasks and show that classical results from mathematical logic -- including Rosser incompleteness, Tarski's undefinability theorem, and Löb's theorem -- imply the persistence of unresolved logical tasks within sufficiently expressive reasoning systems.
  Together these results provide a mathematical perspective on the asymptotic behaviour of expanding generative systems, showing that long-run capability growth is constrained both by diminishing marginal improvements in task coverage and by fundamental logical limitations on internal reasoning.

processed.summary:
* Establishes a **global task-space framework** to mathematically analyze the capability growth of expanding generative systems such as Transformers and Diffusion models.
* Mathematically proves that the **marginal improvement** in task coverage must asymptotically diminish as model capacity increases, suggesting a natural ceiling to scaling.
* Integrates **algorithmic probability** and complexity-weighted hypothesis classes to provide quantitative bounds on prediction performance improvements.
* Links **classical logic theorems** (Godel, Tarski, Löb) to modern AI, demonstrating that sufficiently expressive reasoning systems face inherent, unresolvable logical gaps.
key_results: Marginal probability mass of newly solvable tasks converges to zero as system capacity scales.
Quantitative bounds on prediction performance were established using algorithmic probability frameworks.
Rosser incompleteness implies that expanding systems will always encounter unresolved logical tasks.
Tarski's undefinability theorem limits the internal truth-tracking capabilities of reasoning models.
Löb's theorem suggests fundamental constraints on internal reasoning consistency for expressive agents.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that expanding generative models face inevitable diminishing returns and fundamental logical constraints on reasoning capability.

title: TSegAgent: Zero-Shot Tooth Segmentation via Geometry-Aware Vision-Language Agents
summary: Automatic tooth segmentation and identification from intra-oral scanned 3D models are fundamental problems in digital dentistry, yet most existing approaches rely on task-specific 3D neural networks trained with densely annotated datasets, resulting in high annotation cost and limited generalization to scans from unseen sources. Thus, we propose TSegAgent, which addresses these challenges by reformulating dental analysis as a zero-shot geometric reasoning problem rather than a purely data-driven recognition task. The key idea is to combine the representational capacity of general-purpose foundation models with explicit geometric inductive biases derived from dental anatomy. Instead of learning dental-specific features, the proposed framework leverages multi-view visual abstraction and geometry-grounded reasoning to infer tooth instances and identities without task-specific training. By explicitly encoding structural constraints such as dental arch organization and volumetric relationships, the method reduces uncertainty in ambiguous cases and mitigates overfitting to particular shape distributions. Experimental results demonstrate that this reasoning-oriented formulation enables accurate and reliable tooth segmentation and identification with low computational and annotation cost, while exhibiting strong generalization across diverse and previously unseen dental scans.

processed.summary:
- TSegAgent introduces a **zero-shot geometric reasoning** framework for dental analysis, replacing traditional data-hungry 3D neural networks with **Vision-Language Agents**.
- The architecture utilizes **multi-view visual abstraction** to map 3D scans into formats interpretable by general-purpose foundation models, leveraging their inherent reasoning capabilities.
- By integrating **explicit geometric inductive biases**, such as dental arch organization, the system enforces structural constraints that mitigate ambiguity in tooth identification.
- This approach significantly reduces **annotation overhead** and improves generalization across diverse, unseen datasets compared to supervised 3D segmentation models.
key_results: Achieved accurate tooth segmentation and identification without task-specific training or labeled datasets.
Successfully utilized general-purpose foundation models for specialized 3D medical geometry tasks.
Demonstrated superior generalization capabilities across diverse intra-oral scanner sources.
Significantly reduced computational and human annotation costs versus traditional 3D neural networks.
Mitigated uncertainty in ambiguous anatomical cases through explicit structural constraint encoding.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce TSegAgent, a zero-shot vision-language framework that automates 3D tooth segmentation through geometry-aware reasoning.

title: Ontology-Based Knowledge Modeling and Uncertainty-Aware Outdoor Air Quality Assessment Using Weighted Interval Type-2 Fuzzy Logic
summary: Outdoor air pollution is a major concern for the environment and public health, especially in areas where urbanization is taking place rapidly. The Indian Air Quality Index (IND-AQI), developed by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), is a standardized reporting system for air quality based on pollutants such as PM2.5, PM10), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), sulfur dioxide (SO2), ozone (O3), carbon monoxide (CO), and ammonia (NH3). However, the traditional calculation of the AQI uses crisp thresholds and deterministic aggregation rules, which are not suitable for handling uncertainty and transitions between classes. To address these limitations, this study proposes a hybrid ontology-based uncertainty-aware framework integrating Weighted Interval Type-2 Fuzzy Logic with semantic knowledge modeling. Interval Type-2 fuzzy sets are used to model uncertainty near AQI class boundaries, while pollutant importance weights are determined using Interval Type-2 Fuzzy Analytic Hierarchy Process (IT2-FAHP) to reflect their relative health impacts. In addition, an OWL-based air quality ontology extending the Semantic Sensor Network (SSN) ontology is developed to represent pollutants, monitoring stations, AQI categories, regulatory standards, and environmental governance actions. Semantic reasoning is implemented using SWRL rules and validated through SPARQL queries to infer AQI categories, health risks, and recommended mitigation actions. Experimental evaluation using CPCB air quality datasets demonstrates that the proposed framework improves AQI classification reliability and uncertainty handling compared with traditional crisp and Type-1 fuzzy approaches, while enabling explainable semantic reasoning and intelligent decision support for air quality monitoring systems

processed.summary:
• Proposes a hybrid **uncertainty-aware framework** that integrates **Weighted Interval Type-2 Fuzzy Logic** with OWL-based semantic knowledge modeling for air quality assessment.
• Extends the **Semantic Sensor Network (SSN) ontology** to represent complex relationships between pollutants, monitoring stations, and health-based regulatory standards.
• Implements **SWRL (Semantic Web Rule Language)** and SPARQL queries to enable explainable automated reasoning for health risk inference and mitigation actions.
• Utilizes **IT2-FAHP** (Interval Type-2 Fuzzy Analytic Hierarchy Process) to determine pollutant weights, improving classification reliability near categorical boundaries.
key_results: Demonstrated superior uncertainty handling compared to traditional crisp and Type-1 fuzzy logic approaches.
Successful integration of pollutant importance weights based on relative health impacts using fuzzy logic.
Creation of a formal air quality ontology that facilitates data interoperability and environmental governance.
Validation of explainable semantic reasoning for determining AQI categories and mitigation strategies.
Improved reliability in AQI classification using CPCB datasets compared to deterministic aggregation rules.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Indian research institutions develop an ontology-based fuzzy logic framework to improve air quality assessment through uncertainty-aware semantic reasoning.

title: Unbiased Dynamic Multimodal Fusion
summary: Traditional multimodal methods often assume static modality quality, which limits their adaptability in dynamic real-world scenarios. Thus, dynamical multimodal methods are proposed to assess modality quality and adjust their contribution accordingly. However, they typically rely on empirical metrics, failing to measure the modality quality when noise levels are extremely low or high. Moreover, existing methods usually assume that the initial contribution of each modality is the same, neglecting the intrinsic modality dependency bias. As a result, the modality hard to learn would be doubly penalized, and the performance of dynamical fusion could be inferior to that of static fusion. To address these challenges, we propose the Unbiased Dynamic Multimodal Learning (UDML) framework. Specifically, we introduce a noise-aware uncertainty estimator that adds controlled noise to the modality data and predicts its intensity from the modality feature. This forces the model to learn a clear correspondence between feature corruption and noise level, allowing accurate uncertainty measure across both low- and high-noise conditions. Furthermore, we quantify the inherent modality reliance bias within multimodal networks via modality dropout and incorporate it into the weighting mechanism. This eliminates the dual suppression effect on the hard-to-learn modality. Extensive experiments across diverse multimodal benchmark tasks validate the effectiveness, versatility, and generalizability of the proposed UDML. The code is available at https://github.com/shicaiwei123/UDML.

processed.summary:
- Proposes the **Unbiased Dynamic Multimodal Learning (UDML)** framework to address static quality assumptions and intrinsic dependency biases in multimodal systems.
- Introduces a **noise-aware uncertainty estimator** that forces the model to map feature corruption to noise levels for precise uncertainty measurement in extreme conditions.
- Implements a **weighting mechanism** based on quantified modality reliance bias via **modality dropout**, preventing the dual suppression of hard-to-learn modalities.
- Improves the **robustness and adaptability** of multimodal fusion by dynamically adjusting contributions based on real-time modality quality.
key_results: Eliminated the 'dual suppression' effect on modalities that are inherently harder for networks to learn.
Achieved accurate uncertainty quantification across both extremely low and extremely high noise environments.
Outperformed existing static and dynamic fusion methods across multiple diverse benchmarks.
Successfully decoupled modality quality estimation from empirical metrics through noise intensity prediction.
Demonstrated strong generalizability across various multimodal tasks and architectures.
